1: ʾal-titḥar bammĕrēʿîm ʾal-tĕqannēʾ bĕʿōśê ʿawlâ (ʾal-tiṯḥar bammĕrēʿîm ʾal-tĕqannēʾ bĕʿōśê ʿawlâ): 'Fret not yourself because of evildoers; be not envious of wrongdoers.' Psalm 37 is an alphabetic acrostic (each pair of verses beginning with successive Hebrew letters) attributed to David, addressing the classic theodicy problem of the prosperous wicked. The opening double prohibition — ʾal-titḥar ('do not fret') and ʾal-tĕqannēʾ ('do not be envious') — identifies the spiritual danger: not the wicked's prosperity itself but the psalmist's inner reaction to it. The verb ḥārâ ('to burn, be agitated') in the hitpael describes smoldering indignation; qānāʾ ('to be jealous, envious') describes the coveting of another's success. Both responses misread the situation.
2: kî kĕḥāṣîr mĕhērâ yibbālû ûkĕyereq dešeʾ yibbōlûn (kî kĕḥāṣîr mĕhērâ yibbālû ûkĕyereq dešeʾ yibbōlûn): 'For they will soon fade like the grass and wither like the green herb.' The conjunction kî ('for') provides the theological reason for not fretting: the wicked's prosperity is temporary. The two similes — kĕḥāṣîr ('like grass') and kĕyereq dešeʾ ('like the green herb/plant') — are temporal metaphors of rapid decay. The hithpalel yibbālû ('they will wither/fade') describes the irreversible process; yibbōlûn ('they will wither') is repeated for emphasis. The comparison to grass is the psalm's foundational theodicy: what appears permanent is categorically transient (cf. Psalms 90:5-6; Isaiah 40:6-8; 1 Peter 1:24).
3: beṭaḥ baYHWH waʿăśēh ṭôb šĕkon ʾereṣ ûrĕʿēh ʾĕmûnâ (ḇeṭaḥ ḇaYHWH waʿăśēh ṭôḇ šĕḵon ʾereṣ ûrĕʿēh ʾĕmûnâ): 'Trust in the LORD and do good; dwell in the land and befriend faithfulness.' The psalm's positive prescriptions begin: bāṭaḥ ('trust') and ʿāśâ ṭôb ('do good') are the twin responses to the wicked's prosperity. The promise: šĕkon ʾereṣ ('dwell in the land') — the land-inheritance theme from the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 12:7; Psalms 15:7) is restated as the reward of covenant faithfulness. The phrase ûrĕʿēh ʾĕmûnâ, 'shepherd faithfulness / feed on faithfulness,' uses the verb rāʿâ ('shepherd, graze') — the psalmist is to live off divine ʾĕmûnâ as sheep graze on good pasture.
4: wĕhitʿannag ʿal-YHWH wĕyittĕn-lĕkā mišʾălôt libbĕkā (wĕhiṯʿannag ʿal-YHWH wĕyittĕn-lĕḵā mišʾălôṯ libbĕḵā): 'Delight yourself in the LORD, and he will give you the desires of your heart.' The hitpael wĕhitʿannag ('delight yourself') from ʿānag ('to be soft, delicate, to luxuriate') describes finding YHWH himself as one's deepest pleasure — the enjoyment of God as the supreme satisfaction. The result: wĕyittĕn-lĕkā mišʾălôt libbĕkā, 'he will give you the petitions of your heart.' The phrase is not a blank check for wish-fulfillment but a promise that when YHWH is one's delight, the heart's desires are shaped by that delight — and YHWH fulfills desires so shaped. The heart that delights in YHWH will not desire what YHWH cannot give.
5: gōl ʿal-YHWH darkekā ûbeṭaḥ ʿālāyw wĕhûʾ yaʿăśeh (ḡōl ʿal-YHWH ḏarḵeḵā ûḇeṭaḥ ʿālāyw wĕhûʾ yaʿăśeh): 'Commit your way to the LORD; trust in him, and he will act.' The imperative gōl ('roll!') from gālal ('to roll') — used idiomatically for rolling a burden onto another (Proverbs 16:3) — describes committing the entire way (derek) to YHWH: the path of one's life handed over completely. The ground: ûbeṭaḥ ʿālāyw, 'and trust in him' — trust is the posture that enables the rolling. The result: wĕhûʾ yaʿăśeh, 'and he will act' — the emphatic subject hûʾ ('he himself') emphasizes that YHWH, not the psalmist, will bring it to pass. Human commitment enables but does not produce the outcome.
6: wĕhôṣîʾ kāʾôr ṣidqekā ûmišpāṭekā katssohorayim (wĕhôṣîʾ ḵāʾôr ṣiḏqeḵā ûmišpāṭeḵā katssohorayim): 'He will bring forth your righteousness as the light, and your justice as the noonday.' The hiphil wĕhôṣîʾ ('he will bring forth') uses the light-emergence metaphor: righteousness that is currently hidden or obscured will be revealed. The comparison kāʾôr ('like light') and katssohorayim ('like the noonday') moves from dawn to high noon — the most brilliant, unambiguous illumination. This verse addresses the experience of the righteous person whose innocence is not recognized by the world: YHWH himself will eventually make it as visible as noon light.
7: dōm laYHWH wĕhithôlel lô ʾal-titḥar bĕmatzlîaḥ darkô (ḏôm laYHWH wĕhiṯhôlel lô ʾal-tiṯḥar bĕmaṣlîaḥ darkô): 'Be still before the LORD and wait patiently for him; fret not yourself over the one who prospers in his way.' The verb dōm ('be silent, still') is a rare imperative that requires active quieting — the stillness takes effort when circumstances provoke agitation. The hitpael wĕhithôlel lô ('wait in hope for him') from ḥîl ('to writhe, wait') images the taut waiting of one in labor — expectant but not yet relieved. The return of ʾal-titḥar ('fret not') from Psalms 37:1 (now specifically about the one maṣlîaḥ darkô, 'who prospers in his way') shows the repeated temptation: success without apparent punishment provokes the very fretting the psalm forbids.
8: rĕpēh mēʾap wĕʿăzōb ḥēmâ ʾal-titḥar ʾak-lĕhārēaʿ (rep̄ēh mēʾap wĕʿăzōḇ ḥēmâ ʾal-tiṯḥar ʾaḵ-lĕhārēaʿ): 'Refrain from anger and forsake wrath! Fret not yourself; it tends only to evil.' The triple prohibition — rĕpēh ('cease!'), ʿăzōb ('abandon!'), ʾal-titḥar ('do not fret!') — demands a progressive abandonment of reactive emotions. The word ʾap ('anger, nose') and ḥēmâ ('heat, wrath') together capture both the appearance (flared nostrils) and the inner temperature of anger. The final clause ʾak-lĕhārēaʿ, 'it tends only to evil' — the fretting and anger, left to run their course, lead to evildoing. The unfretting psalmist avoids becoming what he condemns in the wicked.
9: kî mĕrēʿîm yikkārētûn wĕqōwê YHWH hēmmâ yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ (kî mĕrēʿîm yikkārētûn wĕqōwê YHWH hēmmâ yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ): 'For the evildoers shall be cut off, but those who wait for the LORD shall inherit the land.' The kî ('for') gives the ground for the prohibitions of Psalms 37:7-8: the outcome is secure. The verb kārat (niphal, 'be cut off') — used of covenant curses (Genesis 17:14; Leviticus 20:17) — applies to the evildoers. But qōwê YHWH ('those who wait for the LORD') — using the taut-expectation root qāwâ — will yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ ('inherit the land'). This verse is the first of five land-inheritance promises in Psalm 37 (Psalms 37:9,11,22,29,34), which Jesus universalizes in Matthew 5:5: 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.'
10: wĕʿôd mĕʿaṭ wĕʾên rāšāʿ wĕhitbônanttā ʿal-mĕqōmô wĕʾênennu (wĕʿôḏ mĕʿaṭ wĕʾên rāšāʿ wĕhiṯḇônantā ʿal-mĕqōmô wĕʾênennu): 'In just a little while, the wicked will be no more; though you look carefully at his place, he will not be there.' The temporal phrase ʿôd mĕʿaṭ ('yet a little while') frames the wicked's dominance as imminently temporary. The verb wĕhitbônantā (hitpael, 'you will look carefully, consider attentively') describes the careful inspection of the wicked's place (maqôm) — and finding nothing. The disappearance is not merely death but trace-erasure: no evidence of the wicked's tenure remains (cf. the name-blotting of Psalms 34:16). Hebrews 10:37 quotes this phrase: 'in just a little while, he who is coming will come and will not delay.'
11: waʿănāwîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕhitʿannĕgû ʿal-rōb šālôm (waʿănāwîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕhiṯʿannĕgû ʿal-rōḇ šālôm): 'But the meek shall inherit the land and delight themselves in abundant peace.' The noun ʿănāwîm ('meek, afflicted, humble') — those bent down by circumstance or by posture of humility before God — are contrasted with the rāšāʿ ('wicked'). The land inheritance yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ is their promise. The additional blessing: wĕhitʿannĕgû ʿal-rōb šālôm, 'they will delight themselves in abundant peace' — using the same hitpael of ʿānag from Psalms 37:4 ('delight in the LORD'), now applied to šālôm in superabundance. Jesus quotes this verse verbatim in Matthew 5:5, making the Beatitude a direct citation of the Psalter's wisdom tradition. The land promise is re-territorialized to encompass the whole earth (gē, LXX).
12: zōmēm rāšāʿ latsaddîq ûḥōrēq ʿālāyw šinnāyw (zōmēm rāšāʿ latsaddîq ûḥōrēq ʿālāyw šinnāyw): 'The wicked plots against the righteous and gnashes his teeth at him.' The participle zōmēm ('plotting, scheming') from zāmam ('to plan, devise') describes deliberate malice — the wicked's agenda is specifically targeted at (lĕ-) the righteous. The phrase ḥōrēq šinnāyw ('gnashing his teeth') is the bodily expression of rage and contempt, the visible sign of the wicked's inner disposition. The gnashing of teeth as a sign of judgment's approach appears in Matthew 8:12; Psalms 13:42; Psalms 22:13 — the psalmist's image of the wicked's teeth-gnashing becomes, in Jesus's teaching, the eschatological condition of those who refused the kingdom.
13: ʾădōnāy yiśḥaq-lô kî rāʾâ kî yābōʾ yômô (ʾăḏōnāy yiśḥaq-lô kî rāʾâ kî yāḇōʾ yômô): 'But the Lord laughs at the wicked, for he sees that his day is coming.' The divine laughter — YHWH yiśḥaq ('laughs, mocks') — uses the same verb as Psalms 2:4 where YHWH laughs at the nations conspiring against his anointed. The laughter is not cruelty but the expression of incomprehensible superiority: the wicked's plotting (Psalms 37:12) is ludicrous from the perspective of one who sees yômô, 'his day.' The 'day' of judgment coming for the wicked makes all his scheming appear as the futile activity of someone who has already lost.
14: ḥereb pāṭĕḥû rĕšāʿîm wĕdārĕkû qaštām lĕhappîl ʿānî wĕʾebyôn (ḥereḇ pāṭĕḥû rĕšāʿîm wĕḏārĕḵû qaštām lĕhappîl ʿānî wĕʾeḇyôn): 'The wicked draw the sword and bend their bows to bring down the poor and needy.' The wicked's weapons — ḥereb ('sword') and qešet ('bow') — are deployed against ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ('the poor and needy'): those least able to defend themselves. The phrase lĭṭbōaḥ yišrê-derek, 'to slaughter those who walk uprightly,' adds a third target — the morally upright. The combination: the structural underdog (poor/needy) and the moral exemplar (upright) are the wicked's primary victims. Power exploits vulnerability and virtue simultaneously.
15: ḥarbām tābôʾ bĕlibbām wĕqaštôtām tišbārenâ (ḥarḇām ṯāḇôʾ bĕlibbām wĕqaštôṯām tišbārenâ): 'Their sword shall enter their own heart, and their bows shall be broken.' The lex talionis principle operates: ḥarbām ... bĕlibbām, 'their own sword into their own heart' — the weapon aimed at others pierces the wielder. The passive tišbārenâ ('will be broken') — the bows snapping — echoes Psalms 37:17's 'arms of the wicked will be broken.' The reversal: the weapons of aggression become instruments of self-destruction. The wicked's armament, far from securing their dominance, becomes the mechanism of their defeat. This is the Psalter's consistent theology of divine poetic justice.
16: ṭôb mĕʿaṭ latsaddîq mēhāmôn rĕšāʿîm rabbîm (ṭôḇ mĕʿaṭ latsaddîq mēhāmôn rĕšāʿîm rabbîm): 'Better is the little that the righteous has than the abundance of many wicked.' The comparative ṭôb ... mē- ('better than') structure is the classic wisdom saying of comparative value (cf. Proverbs 15:16-17; Psalms 16:8). The mĕʿaṭ ('little, few') of the righteous is better than the hāmôn ('multitude, wealth, noise') of many wicked — not because poverty is virtue but because the righteous man's little is held in covenant with YHWH and therefore is genuinely satisfying, while the wicked's abundance is precarious and morally contaminated.
17: kî zĕrôʿôt rĕšāʿîm tišbārenâ wĕsômēk ṣaddîqîm YHWH (kî zĕrôʿôṯ rĕšāʿîm tišbārenâ wĕsômēk ṣaddîqîm YHWH): 'for the arms of the wicked shall be broken, but the LORD upholds the righteous.' The noun zĕrôʿôt ('arms') denotes power and strength — the arms of the wicked that do violence (cf. Psalms 10:15) will tišbārenâ ('be broken'). The contrast: wĕsômēk ṣaddîqîm YHWH, 'but the LORD sustains/upholds the righteous.' The participle sômēk (from sāmak, 'to lean upon, support') pictures YHWH as the structural support on which the righteous lean their entire weight. The wicked support themselves by their own strength (which breaks); the righteous are upheld by YHWH (who does not break).
18: yôdēaʿ YHWH yĕmê tĕmîmîm wĕnaḥălātām lĕʿôlām tihyeh (yôḏēaʿ YHWH yĕmê ṯĕmîmîm wĕnaḥălāṯām lĕʿôlām tihyeh): 'The LORD knows the days of the blameless, and their heritage will abide forever.' The verb yāḏaʿ ('to know') applied by YHWH to yĕmê tĕmîmîm ('the days of the blameless') is covenantal knowledge: intimate attention and care, not merely information. The word tāmîm ('blameless, complete') — used of Noah (Genesis 6:9), Abraham (Genesis 17:1), and sacrificial animals without defect — describes moral integrity. The result: naḥălātām lĕʿôlām, 'their inheritance forever' — permanence opposed to the transience of the wicked (Psalms 37:2).
19: lōʾ-yēbōšû bĕʿēt rāʿâ ûbîmê rĕʿābôn yiśbāʿû (lōʾ-yēḇōšû bĕʿēṯ rāʿâ ûḇîmê rĕʿāḇôn yiśbāʿû): 'They are not put to shame in evil times; in the days of famine they have abundance.' The promise lōʾ-yēbōšû ('they will not be ashamed/disappointed') ensures the covenant community is not humiliated even bĕʿēt rāʿâ ('in the time of evil/trouble'). The specific test: bîmê rĕʿābôn ('in days of famine') — the ultimate scarcity test — and yet yiśbāʿû ('they will be satisfied/full'). The pair shame/satisfaction frames the issue: shame is the social experience of failure; fullness is its physical opposite. The blameless will experience both social dignity and physical provision even when circumstances are worst.
20: kî rĕšāʿîm yōbēdû wĕʾōyĕbê YHWH kîqar kārîm kālû bĕʿāšān kālû (kî rĕšāʿîm yōḇēḏû wĕʾōyĕḇê YHWH kîqar kārîm kālû ḇĕʿāšān kālû): 'But the wicked shall perish; the enemies of the LORD are like the glory of the pastures — they vanish, like smoke they vanish away.' The antithesis: rĕšāʿîm yōbēdû ('the wicked shall perish/be destroyed') against the heritage of the blameless (Psalms 37:18). The double simile: kîqar kārîm ('like the prize of the meadows / the splendor of the pastures') — momentarily beautiful — then kālû bĕʿāšān ('they vanish in smoke'). The reduplication kālû ... kālû ('they vanish ... they vanish') is emphatic: total, complete disappearance. The wicked's visible glory is the most transient thing imaginable.
21: lōweh rāšāʿ wĕlōʾ yĕšallēm wĕṣaddîq ḥônēn ûnôtēn (lōweh rāšāʿ wĕlōʾ yĕšallēm wĕṣaddîq ḥônēn ûnôtēn): 'The wicked borrows but does not pay back, but the righteous is generous and gives.' The economic behaviour of the two types is their moral signature: the wicked lāwâ ('borrows') but lōʾ yĕšallēm ('does not repay') — the loan becomes a theft. The righteous ḥônēn ('shows grace, is generous') and nôtēn ('gives') — without requiring repayment. The wicked's non-repayment is not merely financial irresponsibility but a moral posture of entitlement; the righteous giving is the embodiment of ḥesed in economic life. Proverbs 19:17 and Luke 6:35 elaborate the theology of generous giving without expectation of return.
22: kî mĕbōrākāyw yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ ûmĕqullālāyw yikkārētû (kî mĕḇōrāḵāyw yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ ûmĕqullālāyw yikkārētû): 'for those blessed by him shall inherit the land, but those cursed by him shall be cut off.' The covenant categories — blessed (bārak) and cursed (qālal) — from the Mosaic covenant (Deut 27-28) are applied: the blessed inherit the land (the Abrahamic promise renewed at Sinai) and the cursed are cut off (the covenant curse). The land-inheritance for the blessed (mĕbōrākāyw) is the fourth occurrence of this promise in the psalm (Psalms 37:9,11,22,29,34), establishing it as the psalm's central hope.
23: mēYHWH miṣʿădê-geber kônnānû wĕdarkô yeḥpāṣ (mēYHWH miṣʿăḏê-ḡeḇer kônnānû wĕḏarḵô yeḥp̄āṣ): 'The steps of a man are established by the LORD, when he delights in his way.' The phrase miṣʿădê-geber ('steps of a strong man') refers to each footfall in the journey of life — YHWH kônnānû ('establishes, makes firm') each step. This is divine providence applied to individual biography: not just the broad strokes of history but each step of the specific person is ordered by YHWH. The qualifier wĕdarkô yeḥpāṣ ('and he delights in his way') — YHWH's delight in the person's way is the condition: the steps of the one who delights in YHWH (Psalms 37:4) are established by YHWH. The two delights are correlated.
24: kî yippōl lōʾ-yûṭal kî YHWH sômēk yādô (kî yippōl lōʾ-yûṭal kî YHWH sômēk yāḏô): 'though he fall, he shall not be cast headlong, for the LORD upholds his hand.' The concessive kî yippōl ('though he fall') acknowledges real failure, stumbling, and setback: the psalm is not naively optimistic. The qualification lōʾ-yûṭal ('he will not be hurled down') — from yāṭal ('to cast, throw') — distinguishes a fall that is recovered from a fatal fall. The reason: kî YHWH sômēk yādô, 'for the LORD holds his hand' — the image of a father holding a child's hand while the child stumbles, preventing a catastrophic fall. The stumbling is real; the destruction is prevented.
25: naʿar hāyîtî gam-zāqantî wĕlōʾ-rāʾîtî ṣaddîq neʾĕzāb wĕzarʿô mĕbaqqeš-lāḥem (naʿar hāyîtî ḡam-zāqantî wĕlōʾ-rāʾîtî ṣaddîq neʾĕzāḇ wĕzarʿô mĕḇaqqeš-lāḥem): 'I have been young, and now am old, yet I have not seen the righteous forsaken or his children begging for bread.' The personal testimony spans a lifetime: naʿar ... gam-zāqantî ('young ... and now old'). The claim wĕlōʾ-rāʾîtî ṣaddîq neʾĕzāb ('I have not seen the righteous forsaken') is empirical wisdom from lived experience — but it is also a theological claim that requires nuance: the psalmist is not denying that righteous people suffer (Psalms 37:19's 'evil times') but that YHWH finally abandons them. The observation is a pattern across a long life, not a promise that every moment will be painless.
26: kol-hayyôm ḥônēn ûmalweh wĕzarʿô lĕbirākâ (ḵol-hayyôm ḥônēn ûmalweh wĕzarʿô lĕḇirāḵâ): 'He is ever lending generously, and his children become a blessing.' The participle ḥônēn ('showing favor, being generous') combined with malweh ('lending') describes habitual generosity: the righteous person's default economic mode is giving and lending without demanding return. The result wĕzarʿô lĕbirākâ, 'and his seed/children become a blessing,' shows the transgenerational effect: the righteous man's character shapes his children into the same pattern, and they in turn are lĕbirākâ ('for a blessing') to others (cf. Genesis 12:2: Abraham 'will be a blessing').
27: sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôb ûšĕkon lĕʿôlām (sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôḇ ûšĕḵon lĕʿôlām): 'Turn away from evil and do good; so shall you dwell forever.' The ethical imperative returns to Psalms 37:3's pattern: sûr mērāʿ ('depart from evil') + ʿāśēh ṭôb ('do good') — the pair appears also in Psalms 34:14 and Isaiah 1:16-17 as the prophetic summary of covenant ethics. The promise: ûšĕkon lĕʿôlām, 'you will dwell forever' — the land-dwelling of Psalms 37:3 extended to perpetuity. The combination of ethical instruction and land-promise reflects the covenant logic of Deuteronomy: obedience produces land-tenure; disobedience leads to exile.
28: kî YHWH ʾōhēb mišpāṭ wĕlōʾ-yaʿăzōb ʾet-ḥăsîdāyw lĕʿôlām nišmārû (kî YHWH ʾōhēb mišpāṭ wĕlōʾ-yaʿăzōḇ ʾeṯ-ḥăsîḏāyw lĕʿôlām nišmārû): 'For the LORD loves justice; he will not forsake his faithful ones. They are preserved forever.' The ground of covenant faithfulness: YHWH ʾōhēb mišpāṭ ('loves justice/right'). The verb ʾāhab ('love') applied to mišpāṭ means YHWH is constitutively committed to just outcomes — it is not a policy but an affection. The covenantal guarantee: wĕlōʾ-yaʿăzōb ʾet-ḥăsîdāyw ('he will not forsake his faithful ones'). The ḥăsîdîm ('covenant-loyal ones') are preserved (nišmārû) forever. The wicked children in contrast: wĕzeraʿ rĕšāʿîm nikrāt, 'the offspring of the wicked shall be cut off' — the generational reversal of Psalms 37:26.
29: ṣaddîqîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕyiškenû lāʿad ʿālêhā (ṣaddîqîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕyiškenû lāʿaḏ ʿālêhā): 'The righteous shall inherit the land and dwell upon it forever.' The fifth land-inheritance promise (Psalms 37:9,11,22,29,34) is stated in its most expansive form: lāʿad ʿālêhā, 'forever upon it.' The permanence is absolute and temporal — not a temporary tenure but everlasting dwelling. The righteous (ṣaddîqîm) — defined throughout the psalm by trust, covenant-keeping, and generosity — are the true heirs of the land against which the wicked falsely claim ownership.
30: pî-ṣaddîq yehgeh ḥokmâ ûlĕšônô tĕdabbēr mišpāṭ (p̄î-ṣaddîq yehgeh ḥoḵmâ ûlĕšônô ṯĕḏabbēr mišpāṭ): 'The mouth of the righteous utters wisdom, and his tongue speaks justice.' The verb hāgâ ('to mutter, meditate, utter') — used in Psalms 1:2 for meditating on Torah — describes the continuous output of the righteous man's mouth: wisdom (ḥokmâ) flows because Torah saturates his inner life. The parallel ûlĕšônô tĕdabbēr mišpāṭ, 'his tongue speaks justice,' connects verbal output with the courtroom: the righteous man's speech produces just outcomes in communal life. Speech and justice are inseparable in covenant ethics.
31: tôrat ʾĕlōhāyw bĕlibbô lōʾ timmāʿēd ʾăšūrāyw (ṯôraṯ ʾĕlōhāyw bĕlibbô lōʾ ṯimmāʿēḏ ʾăšūrāyw): 'The law of his God is in his heart; his steps do not slip.' The phrase tôrat ʾĕlōhāyw bĕlibbô ('the Torah of his God in his heart') is the Psalter's internalization motif — Torah not merely studied externally but lodged in the lēb (will-center). This is the Jeremianic 'new covenant' anticipation (Jeremiah 31:33: 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts') and the Deuteronomic ideal (Deuteronomy 6:6: 'these words shall be on your heart'). The consequence: lōʾ timmāʿēd ʾăšūrāyw, 'his steps do not slip/totter' — internal Torah produces external stability (cf. Psalms 119:11).
32: ṣōpeh rāšāʿ latsaddîq ûmĕbaqqēš lahămîtô (ṣōp̄eh rāšāʿ laṣṣaddîq ûmĕḇaqqēš lahămîtô): 'The wicked watches for the righteous and seeks to put him to death.' The participle ṣōpeh ('watches, lies in wait') describes predatory surveillance — the wicked observing the righteous not with neutral attention but with lahămîtô ('to kill him') as the goal. The surveillance language connects to Psalms 10:8-9 (the wicked lurking) and Psalms 35:4 (seeking the psalmist's life). The righteous person lives under constant hostile observation by the wicked: their virtue provokes hatred rather than admiration.
33: YHWH lōʾ-yaʿazbennû bĕyādô wĕlōʾ yarširʿennû bĕhiššāpĕṭô (YHWH lōʾ-yaʿazbennû bĕyāḏô wĕlōʾ yarširʿennû bĕhiššāp̄eṭô): 'The LORD will not abandon him to his power or let him be condemned when he is brought to trial.' The divine guarantee lōʾ-yaʿazbennû ('he will not abandon him') directly addresses the wicked's lethal intention (Psalms 37:32): the wicked watches to kill, but YHWH will not leave the righteous in the wicked's hand (bĕyādô). The legal protection: wĕlōʾ yarširʿennû bĕhiššāpĕṭô, 'he will not condemn him when he is judged' — the court that the wicked manipulate against the righteous (cf. Psalms 35:11-12) will not produce a verdict of guilty, because YHWH is the ultimate judge.
34: qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ûšĕmōr darkô wîrômĕmĕkā lārešet ʾāreṣ bĕhikkārēt rĕšāʿîm tirʾeh (qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ûšĕmōr ḏarḵô wîrômĕmeḵā lārešeṯ ʾāreṣ bĕhikkārēṯ rĕšāʿîm tirʾeh): 'Wait for the LORD and keep his way, and he will exalt you to inherit the land; you will look on when the wicked are cut off.' The final land-inheritance promise: waiting on YHWH (qawwēh) combined with keeping his way (šĕmōr darkô) produces exaltation and land-tenure. The climactic vision: bĕhikkārēt rĕšāʿîm tirʾeh, 'when the wicked are cut off, you will see it' — the righteous are witnesses to the wicked's end, not victims of it. The psalm's theodicy is resolved in this witnessed reversal.
35: rāʾîtî rāšāʿ ʿārîṣ ûmiṯʿāreh kĕʾezrāḥ raʿănān (rāʾîtî rāšāʿ ʿārîṣ ûmiṯʿāreh kĕʾezrāḥ raʿănān): 'I have seen a wicked, ruthless man, spreading himself like a green laurel tree.' The personal testimony rāʾîtî ('I have seen') introduces an anecdote. The wicked man is ʿārîṣ ('ruthless, terrifying') — one who intimidates through power — and miṯʿāreh ('spreading himself') like a native tree (ʾezrāḥ, 'one born on the land, indigenous') that is raʿănān ('luxuriantly green'). The spreading tree is the picture of robust vitality and established dominance: the wicked man appears as well-rooted and flourishing as any tree in its native soil.
36: wayyaʿăbōr wĕhinnēh ʾênennu waʾăbaqqĕšēhû wĕlōʾ nimṣāʾ (wayyaʿăḇōr wĕhinnēh ʾênennu waʾăḇaqqĕšēhû wĕlōʾ nimṣāʾ): 'Then I passed by, and behold, he was no more; though I sought him, he could not be found.' The narrative suddenly shifts: wayyaʿăbōr ('and he passed by/over') and wĕhinnēh ʾênennu, 'and behold, he was not.' The conjunction wĕhinnēh ('and behold!') signals the surprise of the absence: the psalmist looked for the previously dominant wicked man — waʾăbaqqĕšēhû ('I sought him') — and found nothing. The impressive tree of Psalms 37:35 has vanished. The anecdote confirms the psalm's thesis: the wicked's flourishing is as temporary as it is dramatic.
37: šĕmār tām ûrĕʾēh yāšār kî ʾaḥărît lĕʾîš šālôm (šĕmār tām ûrĕʾēh yāšār kî ʾaḥărît lĕʾîš šālôm): 'Mark the blameless and behold the upright, for there is a future for the man of peace.' The imperatives šĕmār ('mark, observe!') and rĕʾēh ('see!') invite contemplative attention to the tām ('blameless one') and yāšār ('upright one'). What to observe: kî ʾaḥărît lĕʾîš šālôm, 'for there is a future/end for the man of peace.' The noun ʾaḥărît ('end, latter part, future') — usually referring to the final outcome of a life — is positive for the person of šālôm. The man of integrity has a future; the wicked's 'end' is destruction (Psalms 37:38). The contrast of ends reveals which life was worth living.
38: ûpōšĕʿîm nišmĕdû yaḥdāw ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm nikrātâ (ûp̄ōšĕʿîm nišmĕdû yaḥdāw ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm nikrātâ): 'But transgressors shall be altogether destroyed; the future of the wicked shall be cut off.' The antithesis of Psalms 37:37: pōšĕʿîm ('transgressors, rebels') nišmĕdû yaḥdāw, 'are destroyed together' — collective annihilation. The ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm ('future/end of the wicked') is nikrātâ ('cut off') — using the same covenant-curse verb as Psalms 37:9,22,34. The contrast ʾaḥărît šālôm versus ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm is the psalm's sharpest theodicy: the ends of the two lives are qualitatively opposite.
39: ûtĕšûʿat ṣaddîqîm mēYHWH māʿûzzām bĕʿēt ṣārâ (ûṯĕšûʿaṯ ṣaddîqîm mēYHWH māʿûzzām bĕʿēṯ ṣārâ): 'The salvation of the righteous is from the LORD; he is their stronghold in the time of trouble.' The verse states explicitly what the entire psalm has assumed: the righteous do not save themselves. tĕšûʿâ ('salvation, deliverance') is mēYHWH ('from the LORD') — its source is exclusively divine. The noun māʿûz ('stronghold, refuge') from ʿāzaz ('to be strong') describes YHWH as a fortified position — the same imagery as Psalms 27:1 ('the LORD is the stronghold of my life'). The 'time of trouble' (bĕʿēt ṣārâ) is when the stronghold becomes most necessary — and most evident as sufficient.
40: waYHWH yaʿăzĕrēm wayfallĕṭēm yĕfallĕṭēm mērĕšāʿîm wĕyôšîʿēm kî-ḥāsû bô (waYHWH yaʿăzĕrēm wayfallĕṭēm yĕp̄allĕṭēm mērĕšāʿîm wĕyôšîʿēm kî-ḥāsû ḇô): 'The LORD helps them and delivers them; he delivers them from the wicked and saves them, because they take refuge in him.' The four divine actions — yaʿăzĕrēm ('helps them'), wayfallĕṭēm ('delivers them'), yĕfallĕṭēm ('he will deliver them'), wĕyôšîʿēm ('saves them') — are the accumulated deliverances of a full psalm of trust. The final clause kî-ḥāsû bô ('because they take refuge in him') closes the psalm where it began: the foundational act of ḥāsâ-trust (Psalms 37:3, bĕṭaḥ baYHWH) produces the salvation described. Trust initiates; YHWH completes. The acrostic closes with the exhaustive grammar of rescue.
